summary: Today was all about Reynoldstown's big development news and airport chaos. Meanwhile, ICE agents showed up at Hartsfield-Jackson (though folks say they're mostly just standing around), and Atlantic Station somehow scored the top spot on a national 'best places to live' list.

topStory.headline: Reynoldstown's Stalled Beltline Corner Finally Breaks Ground
topStory.summary: After nearly five years of sitting as an empty lot, that cleared corner at Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace in Reynoldstown is finally seeing action. Atlanta-based Embry Development took over from Toll Brothers and is moving forward with 85 residential units just a block from the Eastside Trail. Local folks have been wondering what the heck was happening with that prime spot near Memorial Drive.
topStory.body:
Remember that corner in Reynoldstown where Chester Avenue meets Fulton Terrace? The one that's been sitting empty since they cleared the buildings back in 2021? Well, finally some good news. Embry Development Company has taken over the project from Toll Brothers and is actually breaking ground on what they're calling Camber Crossing.

We're talking 85 residential units (mix of townhomes and condos) just a block west of the Beltline's Eastside Trail and a stone's throw from Memorial Drive. This is exactly the kind of infill development that makes sense in Reynoldstown, especially with all the foot traffic from the trail. The fact that it sat vacant for nearly five years while the neighborhood kept evolving around it was honestly pretty frustrating for locals.

Embry Development's managing partner says they're focused on delivering quality housing that fits the neighborhood's character. Given how hot Reynoldstown has gotten with young professionals and families wanting to be close to the Beltline, these units should move pretty quickly once they're ready. It's about time this corner got the attention it deserved.
topStory.imageAlt: Construction equipment and work beginning at the Reynoldstown development site

stories.headline: Decatur Gets 18 Affordable Cottages in Village-Style Community
stories.summary: Cottages at Midway is getting ready to launch as a 'missing middle' housing solution near downtown Decatur. These 18 standalone houses on Midway Road are designed around shared courtyards and green space, aiming to offer more accessible single-family options in an area where median home values have hit $700,000. It's the kind of thoughtful infill that could help address Decatur's affordability crunch.
stories.body:
With Decatur home prices hitting over $700,000, finding anything approaching affordable in the city limits feels nearly impossible. Enter Cottages at Midway, a new community that's trying to thread the needle on that 2.1-acre site at 2883 and 2893 Midway Road.

This isn't your typical subdivision. We're talking 18 standalone houses arranged around shared courtyards and common green space, what planners call 'village-style' development. The goal is creating community while keeping costs more reasonable than what you'd pay for a renovated bungalow in downtown Decatur. The developers are calling it a soft launch next week, which means they're ready to start drumming up interest.

What makes this interesting is the location. You're close to a major commercial corridor but still in that sweet spot near downtown Decatur. Given how much young families want to be in the Decatur school district, these could move fast once word gets out. It's exactly the kind of 'missing middle' housing that cities like Decatur need more of.
stories.imageAlt: Site map showing the layout of the Cottages at Midway development

stories.headline: West End's GW Parkside Townhomes Start Taking Shape
stories.summary: Those townhomes going up at 600 Hopkins Street in the West End are finally showing some real progress. Ackerman & Co. (the folks behind Lee + White) started this project a year ago and now the first seven units are nearly topped out. These 23 for-sale townhomes are hitting the market this spring, bringing some rare homeownership options right along the Beltline's southwest stretch.
stories.body:
If you've been walking the Beltline's southwest section lately, you've probably noticed the construction at 600 Hopkins Street in the West End. That's GW Parkside (formerly called Hopkins and White Townhomes), and it's finally starting to look like something.

Ackerman & Co., the same developers who've been expanding Lee + White into a legitimate food and office destination, broke ground on this project a year ago. Now we've got seven units nearly topped out, with 16 more to follow. That's 23 total for-sale townhomes, which is pretty rare along this stretch of the Beltline where most new construction has been apartments.

What's interesting is the timing. The West End has been slower to see the kind of development boom that hit other Beltline neighborhoods, but projects like this suggest that's changing. These should hit the market this spring, and given the location right along the trail, they'll probably appeal to folks who want the Beltline lifestyle but don't want to pay Old Fourth Ward prices.
stories.imageAlt: Rendering of the GW Parkside townhomes near the Beltline

stories.headline: Mushi Ni Brings Asian-American Comfort Food to Inman Park
stories.summary: Chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez just opened their first restaurant on Elizabeth Street, right off the Freedom Park Trail. Mushi Ni serves up everything from soft shell crab bao to Peking chicken platters, plus Thai tea martinis. It's the kind of elevated Asian-American comfort food that Inman Park has been missing, and at $25 for a Peking chicken platter that feeds two, the pricing feels right.
stories.body:
After years of pop-ups and working in other people's kitchens, Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez finally have their own place. Mushi Ni opened today on Elizabeth Street in Inman Park, and it's exactly what this stretch needed - serious Asian-American comfort food that doesn't take itself too seriously.

We're talking soft shell crab bao, chicken longanisa bao, umami fries, crab Rangoon puffs, and their signature Peking chicken platters that feed two people for $25. Le and Jimenez both have serious restaurant pedigree (she worked for Gordon Ramsay in New York before moving to Atlanta), and it shows in the execution. The cocktails include Thai tea martinis, which honestly sounds like something that could become a signature drink for the neighborhood.

The location is perfect - right off the Freedom Park Trail where you get foot traffic from both the trail and people heading to and from Little Five Points. Inman Park has had some great food options, but this fills a gap that's been there for a while. Given how much the neighborhood loves supporting local spots, this could become a real anchor for that section of Elizabeth Street.
stories.imageAlt: Interior of Mushi Ni restaurant showing the dining area

stories.headline: Lewis Barbecue's Austin-Style Brisket Lands at Ansley Mall
stories.summary: The smokehouse behind The Cook's Warehouse at Ansley Mall is unlike anything else in Atlanta. John Lewis brought his Austin barbecue expertise (by way of Charleston) to a loft space with panoramic windows overlooking the new Beltline bridge. Six 22-foot smokers run 24/7, turning out some of the best brisket you'll find in the city. This isn't your typical Atlanta barbecue spot.
stories.body:
Most barbecue joints hide their smokers in back alleys or screened-off areas. John Lewis put his upstairs in a spacious loft with floor-to-ceiling windows overlooking the new pedestrian bridge to the Beltline. It's like the fanciest engine room in Georgia, with six 22-foot smokers he helped weld out of 1,000-gallon propane tanks.

Lewis brought his Austin credentials to Atlanta via Charleston, and the difference shows up in every bite of brisket. We're talking about 24-hour smoking schedules, staggered in eight-hour shifts, producing barbecue that tastes like it came straight from central Texas. The location at Ansley Mall puts it right in the heart of that Piedmont/Midtown area where people know good food.

What makes this spot special isn't just the barbecue (though that brisket is legitimately exceptional). It's the whole setup - you can actually watch the smoking process while you eat, and the connection to the Beltline means you're getting foot traffic from all over the city. This feels like the kind of place that could become a destination, not just a neighborhood spot.
stories.imageAlt: Platter of barbecue from Lewis Barbecue showing brisket and sides

stories.headline: ICE Agents Show Up at Airport but Travelers Say They're Just Standing Around
stories.summary: ICE agents are now walking around Hartsfield-Jackson as the partial government shutdown creates massive TSA lines, but travelers and officials can't figure out what they're actually doing. The Trump administration deployed them to help with the chaos, but they're not doing security screenings or immigration enforcement. Meanwhile, TSA agents are working without pay and 41% called out on Sunday, creating wait times of four hours or more.
stories.body:
The scene at Hartsfield-Jackson is pretty surreal right now. You've got ICE agents in tactical gear walking around the terminals while travelers stand in security lines that stretch for hours, and nobody seems clear on what the agents are supposed to be doing. They're not screening passengers, they're not doing immigration enforcement - they're just there.

The numbers tell the story of how bad things have gotten. On Sunday, 41% of TSA agents at the Atlanta airport called out of work. When you're dealing with the world's busiest airport and nearly half your security workforce doesn't show up, you get the kind of chaos we're seeing now. People are reporting four-hour waits just to get through security, and that's on a good day.

Mayor Andre Dickens tried to reassure folks that the ICE agents won't be doing immigration enforcement, but travelers we talked to said they're mostly just standing around. One person put it bluntly: 'I don't think I've ever seen it this busy, and they can't do anything.' It's a perfect example of how political theater doesn't solve actual operational problems. TSA agents need to get paid, period.
stories.imageAlt: ICE agents at Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport

stories.headline: Decatur's West Howard Complete Streets Project Almost Finished
stories.summary: That big Complete Streets makeover along West Howard Avenue in Decatur is finally wrapping up after nearly a decade of planning. The roughly one-mile stretch from Commerce Drive to Paden Circle now has a two-way cycle track with concrete barriers, new sidewalks, and traffic calming features. It's exactly the kind of infrastructure that makes Decatur feel more like a real city and less like a suburban speedway.
stories.body:
If you've been avoiding West Howard Avenue in Decatur because of all the construction, it's time to take another look. The Complete Streets project that started last August is almost done, and the transformation is pretty impressive. We're talking about a full mile from Commerce Drive (near Decatur High School) down to Paden Circle by the East Lake MARTA station.

The big addition is a two-way cycle track with concrete barriers - not just painted bike lanes that cars ignore, but actual protected space for cyclists. They've also redone the sidewalks and added traffic calming features that should slow down the cars that used to treat this stretch like a highway. It's been in the works for nearly a decade, so seeing it actually happen feels pretty significant.

This is the kind of infrastructure project that changes how people move around a city. Instead of West Howard being a car-only speedway, you've got options for walking, biking, and taking transit. Given how much Decatur talks about walkability and sustainability, putting concrete behind those words matters. Plus, the connection to East Lake MARTA means this could become a real commuter route for people biking to the train.
stories.imageAlt: New protected bike lanes and sidewalks on West Howard Avenue in Decatur

stories.headline: City Council Advances Resolutions to Limit ICE Operations
stories.summary: Atlanta's Public Safety Committee unanimously moved forward two resolutions aimed at controlling ICE activities in the city. One would ban ICE detention facilities within city limits, the other would require Atlanta police to document when ICE agents are present during their operations. The full council votes April 20, and dozens of residents showed up to support the measures while also criticizing Mayor Dickens for not signing the firefighter union contract.
stories.body:
The timing couldn't be more relevant. Just as ICE agents started showing up at Hartsfield-Jackson, Atlanta's City Council moved forward with two resolutions designed to limit the agency's local operations. The Public Safety Committee unanimously advanced measures that would ban ICE detention facilities in the city and require APD to document whenever ICE agents are involved in their operations.

Dozens of residents packed the meeting to support these resolutions, but they also used the opportunity to blast Mayor Andre Dickens for refusing to sign the firefighters' union contract that the council ratified almost a year ago. It was the first council meeting since the International Association of Fire Chiefs said Dickens was undermining collective bargaining, and residents made sure he heard about it.

The ICE resolutions head to the full council for a vote on April 20. Given the unanimous committee support and the current situation at the airport, they'll probably pass easily. But the real test will be how the city actually implements these policies, especially with the federal government taking an increasingly aggressive stance on immigration enforcement.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta City Council meeting with residents speaking at podium

stories.headline: Atlantic Station and Midtown Land Top 10 on National 'Best Places' List
stories.summary: Leave it to Atlanta to grab 20% of a national Top 10 list. Atlantic Station scored the #1 spot on Niche.com's 'Best Places to Live in America' rankings, with Midtown coming in at #6. The rankings looked at schools, cost of living, job opportunities, crime, and local amenities across 18,000 neighborhoods nationwide. Not bad for a city that gets constantly underrated.
stories.body:
You know how Atlanta always feels like it doesn't get enough national recognition? Well, here's some vindication. Niche.com just dropped their '2026 Best Places to Live in America' list, ranking over 18,000 neighborhoods across the country, and Atlantic Station grabbed the top spot. Midtown wasn't far behind at #6.

The rankings considered public schools, cost of living, job opportunities, crime rates, and local amenities - basically all the stuff that actually matters when you're deciding where to live. For Atlantic Station to beat out every other neighborhood in the country is pretty remarkable, especially given how much it's evolved from that original corporate development into something that feels like a real community.

What's interesting is having both Atlantic Station and Midtown in the top 10. These are very different neighborhoods that appeal to different types of people, but both offer that mix of urban amenities, job access, and livability that makes Atlanta work. It's also worth noting that both areas have invested heavily in walkability and transit access, which probably doesn't hurt when national ranking sites are looking at quality of life factors.
stories.imageAlt: Atlantic Station development showing modern buildings and walkable streets
